text: Wow, I didn't see this one coming. Out
of all the wannabe summer blockbusters
this year, the last one I expected to
enjoy was a film about a 60-year-old
Brad Pitt in what is arguably the least
exciting motorsport in existence. But
god damn, if this wasn't the most fun
I've had in a movie theater since Top
Gun Maverick. I actually walked out of
this film feeling good about myself and
the world around me. And judging by all
the smiles and the car engines revving
just a little bit louder, so did
everyone else who saw it. F1 is the kind
of big, confident, unapologetically old
school summer popcorn flick that you
just don't see anymore. Filled with
loving shots of engines and computers
and exotic locations, action-packed
races and big spectacular crashes,
intense rivalries and hard one
friendships, grizzled old veterans going
head-to-head with brash young rookies
and refusing to back down. Instead of
serving up some generic corporatized
slop that tries in vain to appeal to
everyone and satisfies no one, F1 knows
exactly who and what its audiences, and
it's happy to cater to them. Anyway, the
movie follows Sunny Hayes, a one-time
Formula 1 prodigy who retired from the
sport after a bad crash that almost
ended his life back in the '90s. After
that, his life kind of spiraled out of
control. And these days, he's a gun for
hire, traveling from place to place and
throwing himself into any race that will
pay the bills. That is until he's
approached by his old friend and former
teammate, Reuben, who wants him to come
out of retirement and race in his
struggling F1 team, Apex. The team has
been coming dead last in almost every
race, and if they don't turn things
around by the end of the season, they're
going to be sold off. Sunny reluctantly
agrees to help, but his return is soon
dismissed as a publicity stunt by the
racing press and his fellow driver
Joshua Pierce, who sees Sunny as a
washed up relic of a bygone age. The two
men soon butt heads and the rivalry
spills over onto the track where they
end up colliding, taking each other out
in their first race together.
[ __ ] But the more they work together,
the more Sunny's hard one experience and
unconventional approach starts to reap
rewards, and Apex actually begins to
move up the rankings. But as rivalries
and resentments continue to simmer, men
and machines are pushed to breaking
point and corporate forces inside the
team conspire against them, can the two
men learn to put aside their differences
and work together to finally get that
podium finish? Now, the plot I'm
describing to you probably sounds pretty
familiar if you've watched Top Gun
Maverick or well, almost any racing
movie ever really, which is interesting
since it was directed by literally the
same guy who did Top Gun Maverick. What
can I say? Joseph Kazinski's figured out
what works, I guess. And maybe that's
the point. I don't need every new movie
to reshape the landscape of film
forever. I just need it to be [ __ ]
good. And here's the thing. F1
absolutely is. It's a simple but
effective story about rivalry and
friendship forged through adversity and
the passing of the torch from one
generation to the next. The cocky,
ambitious young rookie out to prove
himself. And the aging hero risking it
all for one last shot at glory. There's
nothing here that you haven't seen
before, but that doesn't make it any
less enjoyable to watch, especially
because it's been done by people who
actually give a [ __ ] Imagine that in
2025. Sunny and Joshua neatly balance
each other out. Neither man pulled down
or undermined just to elevate the other.
Joshua is the typical rising star, young
and superficially confident, but also
plagued by doubts and insecurities about
the future, wondering if his career
might be over before it's even begun.
Damson Idris does a great job capturing
Joshua's conflicted feelings towards his
teammate and himself. He's a guy still
struggling to figure out who he is and
what he's worth. And instead of being a
shallow antagonist, you actually find
yourself rooting for him as the movie
progresses. Sunny, on the other hand, is
a man at the end of his career, still
determined to prove himself despite his
age, but haunted by the memories of his
past failures. Honestly, it's so [ __ ]
refreshing to see movies that treat
aging characters, especially male ones,
with actual respect instead of turning
them into pathetic scapegoats for
imaginary injustices whose only role is
to shut up, sit down, and move aside. In
the hands of a different creative team,
you just know that Sunny would be
written as some arrogant, outofouch
dinosaur and a clunky example of toxic
masculinity,
probably played by Pedro Pascal.
gay.
But let's not kid ourselves here. This
is absolutely Brad Pitt's movie. He
dominates every second of screen time
like the season pro he is. And I don't
know what kind of pack this guy's made
with the devil, but he somehow seems to
be aging in reverse. Seriously, how the
[ __ ] is this dude in his 60s? You know,
people often point to Tom Cruz as the
last true movie star. And the only
reason Pit sometimes gets overlooked is
because he's never really had a big
franchise or series that he could call
his own, probably through choice if I'm
honest. That's a real shame, though,
because whatever particular combination
of talent and charisma turns you into a
movie star, he's absolutely got it in
spades. On a technical level, I really
can't fault F1. The racing sequences are
superb, making heavy use of real life
tracks, events, and well-known figures
in the sport to add to the realism. It's
nice to see Gunter Steiner in there.
Seriously, look up some of that guy's
interviews. They're [ __ ] hilarious.
How do you sum up your season then this

year?
 It's one short world.
[ __ ] My big worry going into this movie
though was that the actual sport of
Formula 1 racing isn't all that
accessible to the average person,
especially Americans. The cars on the
track go round and round.
It's too clinical, too precise, too
dominated by impenetrable rules and
regulations to make for compelling
drama. But the thing is, this movie
overcomes most of those problems by
working within the constraints of the
sport instead of trying to ignore or
rewrite them, having Sunny and Joshua
exploiting loopholes and technicalities
in each race to give them a tiny
advantage in a sport where tiny
advantages ultimately have a massive
impact. One thing people often overlook
about real F1 teams is how much
cooperation is expected between the two
drivers, with one often expected to
sacrifice position and opportunities to
give the other a better chance. And
yeah, this movie definitely plays fast
and loose with how much [ __ ] they could
actually get away with. But what the
[ __ ] If you want a documentary about F1
racing, go watch Drive to Survive. If I
had to pick out anything that bothered
me, I'd say that the run time is a bit
excessive. Two and a half hours is kind
of bloated for the amount of plot that's
actually going on here. And the
transition from the second to third act
takes longer than it should. I was never
bored exactly, but I did start to wonder
when the movie was going to kick into
high gear again.
Oh [ __ ] I'm doing racing metaphors now.
You know, the older I get, the more my
opinion of guys like Jerry Brookheimimer
is improved. Say what you want about the
bloated macho excesses of the 80s and
'9s, but [ __ ] me. That guy understands
his audience and knows how to make
movies for them. And F1 is a perfect
example of this. It's a film made by and
for men because that's the target demo
for any motorsport movie. And in a
modern Hollywood that's practically
bankrupted itself trying to turn male
oriented brands with universal appeal
into girl brands with zero appeal, guys
like Brookheimimer have the balls to
just stand there and say, "Fuck off. I'd
rather make a movie that some people can
enjoy than generic slop that nobody
enjoys. And you know what? Part of me
wonders if F1 might actually turn out to
be the dark horse in the box office race
this summer. Personally, I always saw it
as a three-way battle between Jurassic
World, Fantastic 4, and Superman. But
considering the strong reviews, the
great word of mouth, and the fact that
even my local cinema was packed out, I'm
wondering now if F1 might just give them
a run for their money. And if so, I'm
[ __ ] here for it. This right here is
exactly the kind of film I want to see
more of. Not another soulless remake or
cashg grab sequel, but an original film
that tells its story, tells it well, and
just like its main character, knows
exactly when to ride off into the
sunsets.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
